hypotheses: Realistic threats posed by COVID-19 will predict greater adherence to public health measures (e.g., social distancing, hygiene) and higher anxiety.
Symbolic threats posed by COVID-19 will predict lower adherence to public health measures and greater engagement in identity-affirming behaviors, as well as associations with psychological distress.

Realistic and symbolic threats are distinct constructs (two-factor structure) and will show different patterns of convergent and divergent validity with related constructs (e.g., need for cognitive closure, beliefs in a dangerous world, Schwartz values).
claim.statement: Realistic and symbolic threats posed by COVID-19 have distinct effects on psychological well-being and public health behavior, with realistic threats increasing compliance and anxiety, and symbolic threats decreasing compliance and encouraging identity-affirming behaviors.
claim.statement_location: Abstract/Introduction (paper overview and aims described in Abstract and Introduction sections)
claim.study_type: Observational (survey-based scale development and validation; cross-sectional and longitudinal)

data.source: Online survey samples recruited via Amazon Mechanical Turk (MTurk)
data.wave_or_subset: March 2020 sample (initial N=399, after exclusions N=346); longitudinal subset: 193 participants with prior data from October 2019
data.sample_size: 346 valid responses in March 2020; longitudinal subsample of 193 participants from October 2019
data.notes: U.S. online MTurk sample; majority White (March 2020: 80.9% White; longitudinal subsample: 85.5% White); mean ages reported (March 2020 Mage = 41.51; longitudinal Mage = 42.27). Data are self-report. Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale is a 10-item measure with items rated on 1-4 (Not a Threat to Major Threat) in at least one study; internal consistencies reported for scales. No publicly shared dataset or code links provided in the document summary.

method.description: The authors developed and validated a 10-item Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale to measure two types of perceived threat (realistic and symbolic). Validation proceeded across multiple studies using exploratory (principal component) and confirmatory factor analyses, tests of longitudinal measurement invariance (configural, metric, scalar), reliability assessments, convergent/divergent validity analyses with related constructs (e.g., need for cognitive closure, beliefs in a dangerous world, Schwartz values), and criterion validity using path/SEM models to examine associations between threat types and psychological outcomes (anxiety, intrusion, avoidance, life satisfaction, affect) and behavioral outcomes (compliance with public health guidelines, identity-affirming behaviors). Samples were collected via MTurk in the U.S., including cross-sectional and longitudinal designs.
method.steps: 1) Develop 10-item Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale items adapted from existing threat measures. 2) Collect online survey data (MTurk) in March 2020 and use earlier October 2019 data for a longitudinal subset. 3) Conduct principal component analysis (Study 1) to explore factor structure. 4) Conduct confirmatory factor analysis (Study 2) comparing two-factor vs. one-factor models and report fit indices. 5) Test longitudinal invariance (Study 3) for configural, metric, and scalar invariance. 6) Compute reliability/internal consistency for scales. 7) Assess convergent and divergent validity via correlations with need for cognitive closure, beliefs in a dangerous world, Schwartz values, personality measures. 8) Estimate path models/SEM to examine associations between threat factors and outcomes (anxiety, intrusion, avoidance, compliance, life satisfaction, affect), including covariates and baseline measures where applicable. 9) Conduct supplemental analyses (e.g., political ideology effects, mediation via identity-affirming behaviors).
method.models: Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA), longitudinal measurement invariance testing, Structural Equation Modeling (SEM)/path models, multiple regression/path analyses.
method.outcome_variable: Multiple outcomes examined including general anxiety (e.g., Beck Anxiety Inventory), COVID-19 impact intrusion and avoidance (adapted Impact of Event Scale), life satisfaction, positive/negative affect, compliance/adherence to CDC guidelines and public health measures, support for public health initiatives, and engagement in identity-affirming behaviors.
method.independent_variables: Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale subscales: 'Realistic threat' and 'Symbolic threat' (latent/observed scores). Political ideology examined in supplemental analyses.
method.control_variables: Baseline anxiety (in longitudinal analyses); outcomes were covaried in SEM models. Other demographic controls (e.g., age, race) are not explicitly detailed in the summary.

results.summary: Evidence supported a two-factor structure (symbolic and realistic threats) of the Integrated COVID-19 Threat Scale across studies, with good reliability and measurement invariance over time. Symbolic and realistic threats showed distinct patterns of associations: realistic threat was more strongly associated with increased anxiety and intrusive thoughts and positively associated with compliance with public health measures; symbolic threat was associated with intrusion and avoidance and was negatively associated with support for and adherence to public health guidelines but positively associated with engagement in identity-affirming behaviors (which in some analyses related to increased positive affect). Longitudinal analyses indicated realistic threat predicted anxiety during COVID-19 when controlling for baseline anxiety, whereas symbolic threat showed different longitudinal associations (e.g., avoidance, intrusion, and some links to anxiety at T2 in some models).

numerical_results.value: Symbolic threat: Eigenvalue=4.20 (42.02% variance); Realistic threat: Eigenvalue=1.64 (16.34% variance)
Full scale D = 0.83; Realistic D = 0.77; Symbolic D = 0.85 (values reported across studies varied slightly)
